steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the text most strongly suggests about southern sea otters in environments where snails, mussels, and clams are present. The passage establishes that snails are energy-poor compared to mussels and clams, and that tool-using otters can access mussels and clams while non-tool-users are limited to snails.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Those otters whose diet consists mainly of snails will likely exhibit less tooth damage than will those otters that use tools to consume mussels and clams.: The passage says the opposite — snail-eaters (non-tool-users) exhibit LESS tooth damage than tool-users. But this is not the strongest suggestion about the energy-environment scenario the question targets. ✗
  • Choice B — Those otters that do not use tools will likely have more robust health than those otters that do use tools.: The passage states that tool-using otters actually gain fitness benefits from higher-quality prey (mussels and clams), so this is contradicted by the text. ✗
  • Choice C — Those otters that do not use tools will likely need to process larger amounts of prey to meet their energy requirements than will those otters that use tools.: Because snails are energy-poor and mussels/clams are energy-rich, otters restricted to snails must eat greater quantities to obtain the same energy that tool-users get from fewer mussels and clams. This follows directly from the passage. ✓
  • Choice D — Those otters that consume mussels and clams without the use of tools will likely spend less time foraging than will those otters that use tools to access the same prey resources.: The passage indicates the opposite — otters limited to energy-poor snails would need to forage more, not less, to meet their energy needs. ✗
C is correct.
